did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning classifiers, specifically k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) and Support Vector Machine (SVM), to classify skin lesions (Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Keratoacanthoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma) from digital images. The method involves feature extraction (higher-order statistics, histogram features, and Image Quality Analysis parameters) and classification using these AI models.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents an original experimental study proposing a new automatic skin diagnosis system. It details a specific methodology involving image preprocessing, segmentation using Color Thresholding in the Lab plane, and feature extraction (higher-order statistics, histogram features, and Image Quality Analysis parameters). The authors trained and evaluated k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) and Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers on a dataset from Dermnet, reporting quantitative results such as F1 scores and accuracy in tables and figures, which characterizes it as an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Skin Lesions Classification Based on Color Plane-Histogram-Image Quality Analysis Features Extracted from Digital Images
brief_summary: This study proposes an automatic skin diagnosis system to classify four types of skin lesions: Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Keratoacanthoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma. The methodology involves preprocessing digital images from the Dermnet database, followed by segmentation using Color Thresholding in the Lab color plane validated by an Interactive Segmentation Tool. Feature extraction includes higher-order statistics, histogram features from RGB, HSV, and YCbCr planes, and Image Quality Analysis parameters. These features are fed into k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) and Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers, with a fusion of their decisions using an OR rule. The evaluation, based on F1 scores and accuracy across various training-testing splits, demonstrates that the proposed method achieves superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods, with a maximum accuracy of 94% for the fused classifier.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the four classes of skin lesions studied and classified using kNN and SVM classifiers on the Dermnet database.

all_task_types.target: Four classes of skin lesions: Malignant Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Keratoacanthoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin lesions using k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) and Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (lesion classes) based on extracted features (higher-order statistics, histogram features, and Image Quality Analysis parameters). Performance is evaluated using F1 scores and accuracy across different training-testing splits.

datasets_used.name: Dermnet database
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of skin disease images collected from the Dermnet website due to the unavailability of benchmarking datasets and permission issues with other databases. The images varied in resolution, illumination, and contrast.

use.training: Used for training classifiers (kNN and SVM) with varying ratios (30%, 40%, 50%, 60%, 70% of samples per class).
use.testing: Used for testing classifiers with the remaining samples (70%, 60%, 50%, 40%, 30% of samples per class) corresponding to the training split.

datasets_used.name: Comparison Dataset (from reference [4])
datasets_used.description: A dataset of 141 images collected from internet resources, used for performance comparison with the method described in reference [4] (Sumithra et al., 2015).

use.testing: Used for performance comparison against the proposed method.
use.other: Used as a baseline for comparison in the study.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate F1 score and Accuracy for performance evaluation.
To repeat experiments with different training/testing splits (30:70, 40:60, 50:50, 60:40, 70:30) to ensure robustness of results.
To measure the average performance of classifiers across different training sample percentages and classes.
To report the range and central tendency of classification accuracy across the 5 trials for each training/testing split.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using F1 scores and accuracy across different training-testing splits, with comparisons made based on the magnitude of these metrics rather than statistical significance.

Dermnet database.metric: Average F1 Score per Class (at 70% training split)
Dermnet database.notes: Fusion of decisions showed better performance for each class compared to individual classifiers.

Comparison Dataset (from reference [4]).notes: This dataset consisted of 141 images collected from internet resources. The proposed method's maximum accuracy (94%) was observed to be better than this comparison method's maximum accuracy (93%).

architecture_used.main_model: k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) and Linear Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers with decision fusion

feature_extraction.segmentation: Color Thresholding in Lab color plane validated by Interactive Segmentation Tool

features.iqa_reference_generation: Gaussian kernel 0.5 applied to input image

classifiers.fusion: OR rule combining SVM and kNN decisions

preprocessing_techniques: Noise removal using Median and Gaussian filters
Hair removal using the DullRazor algorithm
Color space conversion (RGB to HSV, Lab, Luv, YCbCr, NTSc) for feature extraction
Segmentation using Color Thresholding in the Lab color plane
Validation of segmentation using Interactive Segmentation Tool software
Generation of reference image for Image Quality Analysis by filtering input image with a Gaussian kernel (sigma 0.5)
conclusions: The proposed automatic skin diagnosis system, utilizing Color Thresholding in the Lab color plane for segmentation and a combination of higher-order statistics, histogram features, and Image Quality Analysis (IQA) parameters for feature extraction, effectively classifies four types of skin lesions: Malignant Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Keratoacanthoma, and Squamous Cell Carcinoma.

Segmentation using Color Thresholding in the Lab color plane, validated by an Interactive Segmentation Tool, provides better contrast between normal and diseased regions compared to other methods, leading to improved feature extraction.
The fusion of decisions from k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) and Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifiers using an OR rule yields superior performance compared to using either classifier individually.

The proposed method achieved a maximum classification accuracy of 94% and an average F1 score of 0.894 with 70% training samples, outperforming the state-of-the-art method from reference [4] (which had a maximum accuracy of 93%).
Among individual classifiers, the SVM classifier demonstrated better performance than the kNN classifier across the evaluated metrics.
The classification performance varied by lesion type, with the highest accuracy observed for Malignant Melanoma and the lowest for Keratoacanthoma.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the dataset, specifically the unavailability of benchmarking datasets and permission issues, which necessitated the collection of images from the Dermnet database, resulting in images with varied resolution, illumination, and contrast.
important_remarks: The study relies on a non-standard, self-collected dataset from Dermnet due to 'unavailability of benchmarking dataset and permission issues,' which introduces potential population and dataset bias compared to established clinical benchmarks.

The dataset images varied significantly in resolution, illumination, and contrast, creating difficulties for system design and testing, which may limit generalizability to standardized clinical imaging environments.
No external validation was performed; the method was only compared against a baseline from reference [4] using a small subset of 141 images collected from internet resources.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values); performance comparisons are based solely on magnitude of F1 scores and accuracy.
The segmentation validation relies on 'Interactive Segmentation Tool' software, which may introduce subjectivity or inconsistency compared to automated ground truth standards.
Performance varied notably by lesion type, with the lowest accuracy observed for Keratoacanthoma, suggesting potential bias or difficulty in distinguishing this class.
The fusion method uses a simple OR rule to combine SVM and kNN decisions, which is a basic ensemble technique that may not capture complex classifier interactions.
